name: Die Glocke
description: "Die Glocke" (The Bell) is a purported top-secret Nazi scientific technological device, described as a bell-shaped object made of a yet-unknown heavy metal alloy and allegedly researched at a facility near the Wenceslas mine in Ludwikowice Kłodzkie, Poland. The theory surrounding Die Glocke, popularized by Polish journalist and author Igor Witkowski and later Nick Cook in his book "The Hunt for Zero Point," suggests that it was an advanced experiment possibly related to anti-gravity, time travel, or alternative energy sources. Proponents claim the device emitted strong radiation and caused the death of several scientists involved in its research. It is often linked to Nazi occultism and advanced secret weapons programs. Mainstream science and historical analysis offer alternative explanations. No credible evidence supports the existence of Die Glocke as described by Witkowski and Cook. Historians specializing in Nazi Germany and World War II have found no primary source documentation in German archives to corroborate the claims. The alleged harmful effects and secrecy surrounding the project are often attributed to misinterpretations of industrial processes or exaggerations of existing wartime research. The physical descriptions and purported capabilities of Die Glocke are considered pseudoscientific and lack any basis in established physics. Skeptics argue that the story of Die Glocke is likely a combination of misremembered anecdotes, Cold War anxieties, and the desire to sensationalize Nazi technological advancements.
subtitle: Alleged Nazi Wonder Weapon Conspiracy Theory

claims.claim: Die Glocke was a Nazi anti-gravity device.
claims.evidence: Witness testimonies and alleged leaked documents suggest the device warped space-time.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence exists, and alleged documents are likely forgeries or misinterpretations of unrelated research. Anti-gravity is not supported by existing physical models.

claims.claim: The device emitted dangerous radiation that killed scientists.
claims.evidence: Reports of scientist deaths and protective measures taken during experiments.
claims.criticism: Industrial accidents and exposure to known hazardous materials are more plausible explanations. No unusual radiation signatures have been detected in the area.

claims.claim: Die Glocke disappeared at the end of the war, possibly taken to South America or the United States.
claims.evidence: Speculation based on the lack of remains and alleged sightings of similar devices.
claims.criticism: No verifiable evidence supports this claim. The story is likely a product of Cold War anxieties and conspiracy theories about Nazi escape routes.

keyFigures.name: Igor Witkowski
keyFigures.role: Popularized the theory in his writings.

keyFigures.name: Nick Cook
keyFigures.role: Further promoted the theory in 'The Hunt for Zero Point'.

keyFigures.name: Joseph P. Farrell
keyFigures.role: Advocate for the theory relating it to esoteric Nazi beliefs

keyFigures.name: Michael Shermer
keyFigures.role: Skeptic who has debunked many conspiracy theories related to Nazi technology.

timeline.event: Alleged period of Die Glocke research and experimentation.
Igor Witkowski publishes his book, popularizing the theory.
Nick Cook's 'The Hunt for Zero Point' further spreads the Die Glocke myth.

sources.author: Henry Stevens
sources.work: Hitler's Flying Saucers: A Guide to German Flying Discs of the Second World War
